coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots enhanced graphene/carbon nanotubes polyurethane hybrid nanocomposites
coredata.dc:creator: Navidfar, Amir
Peker, Makbule Irmak
Budak, Esranur
Unlu, Caner
Trabzon, Levent
coredata.dc:description: The outstanding dispersion of nanofillers in polymer matrices is needed to fabricate high-performance nanocomposites. Herein, three-dimensional nanostructures of graphene nanoplatelets (GNP) and carbon nanotubes (CNT) with enhanced dispersion quality accompanied by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are prepared within the polyurethane (PU) matrix. CQDs and nitrogen-rich carbon quantum dots (N:CQD) were facilely synthesized using a domestic microwave oven within minutes to improve the interfacial bonding of carbon nanofillers with the polymer matrix, which positively affected the final performance of nanocomposites. Being highly prone to forming bindings with other carbon nanofillers, CQDs restrain graphene stacking and CNT bundling, leading to considerably improving the dispersion quality in the PU matrix to enhance desired properties. PU with binary CNT/CQD and ternary GNP/CNT/CQD nanocomposites revealed higher mechanical properties and thermal stability, in which tensile strength and elastic modulus of PU enhanced up to about 54% and 71% at a very low concentration of 0.25 wt%, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocomposites
Carbon nanotubes
Graphene
Carbon quantum dots
Hybrids

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast orcid primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of CQD synthesis and nanocomposite fabrication.

Photophysical characterization of CQDs and N:CQDs excitation dependent emission of CQDs (a) and N:CQDs (b). Photoluminescence spectrum at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm (PL, black) photoluminescence excitation spectrum at an emission wavelength of 455 nm (PLE, red) and absorption spectrum (blue) of CQDs (c) and N:CQDs (d). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
FTIR spectrum of (a) CQDs and (b) neat and nanofiller reinforced PU foams.
Survey spectra of (a) single, (b) binary, (c) ternary nanofiller compositions and (d) neat PU and its nanocomposites with CNT/CQD and CNT/GNP/CQD.
C1s spectra of (a) pristine, (b) CNT/CQD and (c) CNT/GNP/CQD reinforced PU foams.
Structure of web-like CNT/GNP/CQD hybrids and CQDs on the surface of GNPs and CNTs.
SEM images of nanocomposite foams (a) PU/CNT-GNP-CQD and (b) PU/CNT-CQD (0.25 wt%).
Stress-strain curve of PU and its nanocomposites.
Mechanical properties of nanocomposites as (a) tensile strength, (b) Young's modulus, (c) elongation at breakage and (d) hardness.
CNT/CQD interaction as a debundling mechanism and 3D web-like structure consisting of GNPs, CNTs and CQDs.
(a) TGA and (b) DTG curves of neat and CQD, CNT-CQD and CNT-GNP-CQD reinforced PU foams.
Degradation rates at (a) 250 °C, (b) 400 °C and (c) 600 °C and (d) temperature at 5% degradation.
Properties of H 1710/06 polyol and isocyanate.
C/O ratios obtained from the survey spectra.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots enhanced graphene/carbon nanotubes polyurethane hybrid nanocomposites

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Mechanical Engineering, Istanbul Technical University, Istanbul, Turkey

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Mechatronics Engineering, Bahcesehir University, Istanbul, Turkey.
Corresponding author. MEMS Research Center, Istanbul Technical University, Istanbul, Turkey.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The outstanding dispersion of nanofillers in polymer matrices is needed to fabricate high-performance nanocomposites. Herein, three-dimensional nanostructures of graphene nanoplatelets (GNP) and carbon nanotubes (CNT) with enhanced dispersion quality accompanied by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are prepared within the polyurethane (PU) matrix. CQDs and nitrogen-rich carbon quantum dots (N:CQD) were facilely synthesized using a domestic microwave oven within minutes to improve the interfacial bonding of carbon nanofillers with the polymer matrix, which positively affected the final performance of nanocomposites. Being highly prone to forming bindings with other carbon nanofillers, CQDs restrain graphene stacking and CNT bundling, leading to considerably improving the dispersion quality in the PU matrix to enhance desired properties. PU with binary CNT/CQD and ternary GNP/CNT/CQD nanocomposites revealed higher mechanical properties and thermal stability, in which tensile strength and elastic modulus of PU enhanced up to about 54% and 71% at a very low concentration of 0.25 wt%, respectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hybridization of carbon-based nanofillers with various geometries has shown a synergistic effect in efficiently improving the mechanical, thermal and electrical properties of polymer nanocomposites [1–8]. Hybrid nanofillers inclusion leads to a unique architecture to overcome dispersion challenges, which cannot be achieved using single nanofillers [9–19]. Recently, hybrid nanocomposites using geometrically dissimilar carbon nanofillers such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene oxide (GO) and graphene nanoplatelets (GNPs) have been developed in different polymeric materials [20–24]. Sharma et al. [20] experimentally studied dynamic and static mechanical properties of aramid fiber using GO, CNT and GO/CNT hybrids. Their results showed that aramid-reinforced polycarbonate composite with 0.2 wt% of CNT, GO, and GO/CNT increased tensile strength by about ∼20%, ∼26% and ∼32%, respectively. The hybrid GO/CNT nanofillers revealed a synergistic load transfer along with superior interfacial adhesion. In our previous work, an outstanding synergistic effect between graphene and CNTs was profoundly investigated, and which tensile strength of PU with CNT/GNP hybrids improved by about 43%, relative to 19% and 17% for PU with single CNTs and GNPs, respectively. This is due to the increased contact surface area within hybrid nanofillers from zero-dimension (0-D) to one-dimension (1-D) contact. He et al. [25] stated that the thermal conductivity was improved with three-dimensional (3-D) hybrid GNPs/CNTs in the polymer matrix at very low nanofiller loading, originated by the bridging of the planar graphene by the flexible CNTs.

Recently, abundant advances have been made in carbon-based quantum dots (CQDs). As a new type of 0D carbonaceous nanomaterials with dimensions less than 10 nm, CQDs have attracted great attention due to their quantum confinement, rich surface functional groups, good biocompatibility, water solubility and edge effects, leading to wide applications [26–30]. CQDs are not atomically smooth due to their surface functional groups that assist their interaction with other moieties in a molecule-like manner. This means CQDs may not form agglomerations in the polymer matrix as certainly as larger carbon materials tend to Ref. [31]. The CQDs may have a higher tendency for load transferring owing to the interfacial bonding with other matrices, which may positively affect the performance of nanocomposites [32,33]. Seibert et al. [31] revealed that CQDs improved the mechanical and thermal conductivity properties more effectively than GNPs due to their size and dispersibility.

Hybrid nanocomposites using GO, GNP and CNTs showed a synergistic effect to enhance desired properties of polymeric nanocomposites, however, the dispersion challenge is a common obstacle and improving the mechanism is generally based on forming homogenously dispersed hybrid structures and achieving higher interaction areas between nanofillers and the matrix. This challenge can be overcome by incorporating the CQDs into polymeric nanocomposites. The study by Sreenath et al. [34] focused on the enhancement of carboxylated acrylonitrile butadiene latex (XNBR) latex using hybrid CNTs/CQDs. They sought for excelling in nanofillers dispersion to achieve strong reinforcement through debundling CNTs with carbon dots up to 4 parts per hundred (phr). Another work by Sreenath et al. [35] indicated that XNRB with covalently hybridized CQDs/GO has higher mechanical properties and thermal stability in comparison with XNBR/CQDs and XNBR/GO nanocomposites. So far, no effort has been made to recognize the synergistic effect of CQDs on ternary CNT/GNP hybrid nanocomposites to improve desired properties to the best of our knowledge.

In this work, a rapid bottom-up method is used to synthesize CQDs and nitrogen-rich CQDs (N:CQD) through a domestic microwave oven, which has paved the way for their usage in polymeric nanocomposites [36,37]. CQDs and N:CQDs were used to understand the potential of 0-D nanofillers inclusion in hybrid nanocomposite while forming 3-D structures. A comparative study in terms of tensile properties, hardness and thermal stability of CNTs, GNPs, CQDs and their binary and ternary hybrids within the PU matrix was done. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) have been also studied in detail.

PU foams were formed by polymerization reaction between isocyanate and polyol with a weight ratio of 1:1.2 as the manufacturer (PLUSKIM) recommended. The properties of polyol and isocyanate are summarized in Table 1 .

Both kinds of QDs were facilely synthesized through a microwave-assisted method, which was reported in detail elsewhere [36,37]. Briefly, 1 g of citric acid monohydrate was added into 1 ml ultrapure water and was placed into a domestic microwave oven and heated at 800 W for 12 min to synthesize CQDs (Fig. 1 ). For the synthesis of N:CQDs, 100 mg of citric acid and 100 mg of urea were dissolved in 10 ml ultrapure water and placed into the microwave at 800w for 7 min. The solid products were dissolved in ultrapure water again and were filtered twice. As specified, TEM characterizations demonstrated that synthesized CQDs have a pore size distribution between 2 and 4 nm [36].

Commercially available MWCNTs were synthesized by chemical vapor deposition and have an average diameter of 8–10 nm, an average length of 1–3 μm, a specific surface area of 290 m2/g and purity above 92%. GNPs have a diameter of 1.5 μm, a thickness of 3 nm and a specific surface area of 750 m2/g. MWCNTs and GNPs are both purchased from Nanografi Co. Ltd. Table 2 represents a clear list of nanofiller properties.

The synthesis of PU hybrid nanocomposite foams took place after the functionalization of MWCNTs using hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), which was reported elsewhere [38,39]. Our previous study resulted in the conclusion of 0.25 wt% being the optimal nanofiller ratio for CNT and GNP hybrid reinforced PU foam [12]. Therefore, samples of single, binary and ternary hybrid nanofillers reinforced PU foam are synthesized with the 0.25 wt% concentrations. As there are four types of nanofillers including two types of CQDs, GNPs and CNTs, 12 types of PU and its nanocomposites are fabricated as detailed in Table 3 .

The two-step fabrication of PU foam enables the incorporation of nanofillers before the polymerization process that is initiated by mixing polyol and isocyanate. Thus, it is possible to blend the nanofillers with a polyol to obtain primal conjugations. Nanofillers were added to polyol using a disposable cup to be stirred at 200–2000 rpm for 5 min, exposed to ultrasonic dispersion for 5 min in an ultrasonic bath to be stirred again for another 5 min at 2000 rpm. Isocyanate was added as a final step of stirring for 20 s, then the mixture was poured into a closed wooden mold until finally solidified for about 5 min as Fig. 1 represents. The closed mold in this study allows the elimination of the slicing step for a proper thickness of 10 mm since the mold has convenient dimensions.

At least three tensile specimens for each nanocomposite are cut using a desktop CNC machining according to ISO 1926 standards for tensile testing of rigid cellular plastics. Each specimen is tested using a Shimadzu UTS machine with a 1 kN load cell and under a strain rate of 5 mm/min at room temperature. Samples are first measured in means of width and thickness to calculate stress and strain after testing. Each nanocomposite is subjected to Shore-0 hardness tests using an XF hardness tester on at least eight points. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of CQDs and PU hybrid nanofiller foams were obtained within the range of 4000-650 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1 using a Thermo Scientific iS10 at room temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were conducted in a Thermo Scientific K-alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer system with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source. Dispersion characteristics of nanofiller compositions are investigated with the aid of Hitachi HighTech HT7700 transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Thermal stability of hybrid nanocomposite foams is investigated by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) under nitrogen flow of 100 mL/min on a Q600 by TA Instruments with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 from 30 °C to 700 °C.

The absorption spectrum of CQDs was recorded by Scinco Neosys-2000 single beam ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence (PL) Emission spectrum of each quantum dot was recorded by the Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrofluorimeter by using excitation wavelength (λexc) varying between 350 nm and 450 nm. The photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectrum of each solution was recorded at the emission wavelength of 450 nm. Each sample was dissolved in 10 ml of ultrapure water after purification and diluted until optical density was under 0.1 to prevent errors resulting from self-absorption. All optical characterizations were performed on aqueous solutions of QDs. All spectroscopic analyses were conducted at room temperature.

Detailed characterizations of synthesized CQDs and N:CQD were reported elsewhere [36,37]. CQDs and N:CQDs showed typical photophysical properties of carbon dots which were synthesized in literature before [36,37]. Each carbon dot displayed excitation dependent emission feature, which showed that the surface of each type of carbon dots possessed carboxylic groups. The emission intensity of CQDs rapidly decreased upon an increase in excitation wavelength and almost completely diminished with excitation at 450 nm. However, the emission intensity of N:CQDs remained relatively high at longer excitation wavelengths which was due to the existence of nitrogen in the carbon dot structure and amine groups on the surface of N:CQDs [36,37]. The effect of nitrogen on photophysical properties on carbon dots was observed also in the position of emission peak, photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectrum and absorption spectrum for each carbon dot. CQDs have an emission peak at 457 nm upon excitation at 350 nm, Fig. 2 . As nitrogen was added to the carbon dot structure, the emission peak shifted slightly towards the higher energetic region, 440 nm at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, which is the result of changes in energy states in carbon dots due to heteroatom doping [36,37]. CQDs possessed typical absorption peaks of carbon dots; π-π* transition at 230 nm due to the C=C bond and an n – π* transition around 300 nm due to the C=O bond. However, N:CQDs possessed an n – π* transition peak around 330 nm due to nitrogen within the crystal structure and an extra peak at 410 nm, which is attributed to crystal band-edge transition, due to the smoother crystal structure of N:CQDs [36,37]. It should be noted that the PLE spectrum of CQDs and N:CQDs were completely different as well; the PLE spectrum of CQDs had a single peak at 350 nm with full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 80 nm. On the other hand, N:CQDs had two peaks at 235 nm (FWHM: 50 nm) and 360 nm (FWHM: 60 nm). This result suggested that radiative transitions in N:CQDs can be triggered at lower wavelengths compared to CQDs and nitrogen addition to CQDs completely alter all the electron energy states in carbon dots, which was a typical outcome and was observed in literature frequently [36,37].

Carbon dots have typical infrared bands for carboxylic groups, hydroxyl groups and –CH bonding. In the FTIR spectrum of CD, the peak at 1701 cm−1 was attributed to C=O stretching vibrations in carboxylic groups. The peak at 1410 cm−1 was attributed to CH2 bending vibrations and the broadband around 1100 cm−1 was attributed to O–C stretching vibrations. The broad peak at 3100 cm−1 was attributed to –OH stretching vibrations. In the FTIR spectrum of N-doped carbon dots, the peak at 1608 cm−1 was attributed to C–N stretching vibrations, whereas the peaks at 3450 cm−1 and 3350 cm−1 were attributed to –NH2 peaks. Due to the very low concentration of nanofillers within the PU matrix, the FTIR spectra of CNT, CQD, CNT/CQD, and CNT/GNP/CQD reinforced PU foam do not differ from the pristine PU foam as in Fig. 3 . The transmittance bands that occurred between 3500 and 3200 cm−1 are –NH and –OH stretching vibrations while those in between 3000 cm−1 and 2800 cm−1 are symmetric and asymmetric stretching modes of CH2 groups. Additionally, CH2 scissoring vibrations are identified by the peaks between 1480 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 and the smaller peaks around 1360 cm−1 are attributed to the CH2 wagging vibrations. There are also distinctive peaks between 1710 and 1750, 1600 and 1530 cm−1 attributed to C=O stretching, C=C stretching and coupling of –CN stretching and –NH bending respectively [38]. FTIR spectra of all PU nanocomposite foams have transmittance bands identical to those of the spectrum of pristine PU foam, which is associated with the low amounts of nanofillers as well as unaffected segmented or the chemical structure of the PU foam [12].

The XPS survey scan of CQD, N:CQD, CNT, GNPs and their hybrids are examined along with PU with CNT/CQD and CNT/GNP/CQD. Fig. 4 represents the obtained results for survey scans of nanofiller compositions, PU and its hybrid nanocomposites. The spectra of each nanofiller composition involve typical peaks around 300, 420 and 540 eV for C1s, N1s and O1s, reciprocally, as well as Auger electron peaks as a result of relaxation of ionized atoms [40]. The latter peaks that arise around 950 eV and 1250 eV are O KLL series considered as the result of relaxation phenomena. The only nanofiller composition that displays a peak around 420 eV for nitrogen atoms is the hybrid nanofiller composition of CNT/GNP/N:CQDs as expected since N:CQD is the only material that involves nitrogen. On the other hand, all the materials exhibit typical peaks for carbon and oxygen inclusion with different intensities. Table 4 represents the carbon to oxygen ratios of each material. CQDs have a significant amount of oxygen compared to other materials, which also indicates having a higher number of oxygen-based functional groups on the surface compared to carbon-based groups. For CNT having the highest ratio between carbon and oxygen atoms, the CNT/CQD hybrid has the lowest ratio in comparison to other hybrids; indicating a higher number of bindings between CNTs and CQDs [34]. Ratios belonging to hybrids of CQDs are generally low, especially when compared to the hybrids of CNT/GNP, which imply the extraordinary synergistic effect of CQDs in forming hybrid nanocomposite structures and may positively affect the performance of nanocomposites [32]. Survey spectra of pristine, CNT/CQD and CNT/GNP/CQD reinforced PU foams are illustrated in Fig. 4(d). Spectra of PU foams show typical peaks for C1s, N1s and O1s as well as Auger peaks at 300, 420 and 540 eV and around 950 eV and 1250 eV, respectively [40]. CNT/CQD reinforced PU foam has lower intensities of C1s and O1s peaks in comparison with the ternary hybrid nanocomposite. The C/O ratios of PU and its nanocomposites are all below 1 while the binary hybrid nanocomposite shows the lowest amount.

Fig. 5 represents the C1s spectra of all single and hybrid nanofillers. While an obvious peak around 288 eV for most of the materials can be seen, there is a shift in CNT/CQD composition towards higher energy as a result of stronger coupling [41]. The intensity belonging to the C=O peak of CNT/CQD hybrids has a sharp decrease when compared to one of the pristine CQDs which is attributed to the reduced amount of –COOH groups due to covalent bonding [34].

XPS measurements were conducted to determine the surface composition and chemical states of elements using high-resolution C1s spectra for pristine PU foam as well as its binary and ternary hybrids nanocomposites, as shown in Fig. 6 . C–O and C–C/C=C bonding rate of CNT/CQD reinforced PU foam has a significant decrease while the C=O count is higher in comparison to other materials. The shift for PU/CNT-CQD composition towards higher energy proves the stronger coupling [41]. C1s spectra of PU/CNT-CQD disclose a new peak at 291.36 eV ascribed to N–C=O and at 292.3 eV for π-π conjunction. Also, a peak at 288.5 eV points towards reduced availability of –COOH group resultant from the conjugation process revealed for PU/CNT-GNP-CQD. The peak shifting of 1.4 eV is observed from 287.4 for C=O (COOH) in PU to 288.8 eV for O–C=O in PU/CNT-CQD due to extra bond formations [34]. Therefore, XPS results show the successful bonding of PU with CNT/CQDs and CNT/GNP/CQDs.

TEM observations of CNTs, GNPs, CQDs and their hybrids are illustrated in Fig. 7 . The GNP and MWCNT presence lead to a web-like structure that is almost homogeneously dispersed with the aid of CQDs. The web-like structure of CNT/GNP/CQDs indicates the possibility of a potential enhancement mechanism by the synergy among nanofillers. The CNTs and GNPs are prone to bundling and restacking while CQDs have the potential to create a 0-D roughness structure on the surface of CNTs and GNPs to improve their dispersion. It means that GNPs stacking and CNTs bundling occur rarely in comparison with the environment without CQDs.

Fig. 8 demonstrates SEM images of PU hybrid nanocomposites with 0.25 wt% nanofiller content, which provide evidence of foams cellular microstructures and a series of microstructures focused on the strut area, where carbon nanofillers are located. Although GNPs and CNTs are visible in the images, quantum dots cannot be seen due to their small sizes [42]. Although the surface is a little rougher in some figures, there is no conspicuous aggregation that can be observed. Most carbon nanotubes are well dispersed through the matrix in both figures. The interface between nanofillers and matrix is improved by cross-linking CNT/CQDs with polymer chains [43, 44].

Tensile properties are obtained by calculation after observing the breakage characteristic of specimens to examine the mechanical properties of PU nanocomposites reinforced with four kinds of carbon nanofillers at 0.25 wt%. From our previous work, significant improvement in mechanical properties of PU has been found by incorporation of 0.25 wt% of carbon nanofillers (optimum concentration). The stress-strain curves of PU with binary and ternary nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 9 . Although the tensile strain decreased due to the incorporation of nanofillers, the tensile stress and Young's modulus increased, especially for PU with CNT-CQD and CNT-GNP-CQD.

Average stress and strain values of each nanocomposite are calculated to obtain the tensile strength, Young's modulus and strain at break as in Fig. 10 . The average strength of each nanocomposite are increased with the incorporation of carbon nanofillers relative to pristine PU. Single nanofiller included nanocomposites showed moderate improvement in tensile strength up to about 35% for PU/MWCNTs and in modulus up to about 50% for PU/CQD nanocomposites. The reinforcement efficiency of binary and ternary nanocomposites is superior to that of a single nanofiller system, which approves the synergistic effect among carbon nanofillers. The tensile strength and modulus of binary CNT/CQD nanocomposites indicated the highest improvement of 54% and 71% when compared to pure PU, respectively. On the other hand, tensile strength and modulus values of PU with ternary CNT/GNP/CQD increased by about 47% and 61%, respectively. Ternary hybrids with N:CQD has the least improvement in tensile strength (∼20%) while having one of the highest moduli (59%). While the average results of CNT/CQD and CNT/GNP/CQD hybrids are two of the most outstanding values, all other nanofillers showed moderate improvement. The tensile properties enhancement indicates better dispersion of hybrid nanofiller systems of CNT/CQD and CNT/GNP/CQD as well as increased interfacial interactions between nanofillers and the matrix. The synergistic effect in binary and ternary hybrid nanocomposites is due to improved interaction between CNTs/GNPs and PU matrix through CQD incorporation, resulting in considerably enhanced tensile properties. CQDs possess hydroxyl and carboxylic groups on the surface which strengthen the synergistic effect of carbon dots on self-assembly [33], however N:CQDs also have amine groups on the surface as a result the interfacial interaction becomes significantly disrupted. More efficient stress transfer was obtained via CQDs-assisted hybrid nanocomposites, in which the large surface area and numerous interaction points between the polymer matrix and the hybrid nanofillers are constructed. Carbon nanofillers inclusion within PU matrix has led to a decrease in strain values while the CQD/CNT combination presented the lowest reduction (Fig. 10(c)). The decrease of elongation at breakage indicates a lower toughness introduced by GNPs.

As expected, Shore 0 hardness values of nanocomposites are compatible with results of tensile strength. The highest hardness value is obtained for the CNT/CQD hybrids, followed by CNT/GNP/CQD hybrids as in Fig. 10(d). The progression in the hardness is attributed to increased interfacial interaction between the nanofillers and the PU matrix. Comparatively lower hardness values at the same weight percentages can be ascribed to an uneven dispersion by the formation of agglomerations. Therefore, the results indicate better dispersion characteristics for CNT/CQD and CNT/GNP/CQD filled nanocomposites.

The bundles of MWCNTs caused by π-π interactions are proved to be unbundled with the inclusion of CQDs, as represented in Fig. 11 . Ternary hybrid nanocomposites create 3D structures supported by an increased number of crosslink points due to the presence of CQDs, reducing the stacking of 2D GNPs and increasing the interfacial area to improve overall properties [33]. The desired 3D structure of the nanofillers, as mentioned earlier, is represented in Fig. 11. CQDs amplified the improvement obtained by binary CNT/GNP hybrids. Results indicated a synergistic impact of CNT, GNP and CQD nanofillers, while CNT/CQD hybrids have reached significant improvement, indicating a more potent synergy among nanofillers. As also supported by measured hardness values of all nanocomposites, such improvements indicate better dispersion and higher interfacial interactions with the matrix than other nanocomposites. Although the tensile and hardness behavior of CNT/GNP/CQD hybrids improved compared to CNT/GNP in our previous study [12], the ternary hybrid CNT/GNP/CQD fell behind the binary hybrid of CNTs/CQDs.

The thermal stability of pristine and nanofiller reinforced PU foam are examined using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). Out of all the synthesized nanocomposites, those involving CQDs are examined since hybrids of CQDs have shown the best properties. According to the literature; the degradation process is altered and the initial degradation is suspended due to higher interfacial interactions between the polymer and nanofillers [42]. The improved thermal conductivity increases the residue since it complements the heat dissipation mechanism, and polymer chains located close to nanofillers degrade slower. The results of TGA are investigated in means of temperatures for 5% and 50% weight loss in addition to weight loss at different temperatures. The inset charts in Fig. 12 (a) provide a clear representation of the points for 50% degradation and the temperature of 600 °C. Nanocomposites reinforced with CQD, CNT/CQD and CNT/GNP/CQD hybrids require higher temperatures for 50% degradation compared to the neat PU. The degradation amounts of nanocomposites at temperatures as high as 600 °C increase with the order of CNT/CQD, CQD and CNT/GNP/CQD while neat PU foam has a lower rate of degradation in comparison to CNT/GNP/CQD reinforced PU foam while also being highly close to the rate of CQD reinforced PU foam. In conclusion, the thermal stability of CNT/CQD reinforced PU foam has shown the highest overall improvement in TGA. The highest residue of CNT/CQD reinforced PU foam is attributed to better interaction between nanofillers and the matrix. More efficient heat dissipation and altered heat concentration can be obtained by the formation of CNT-CNT networks through CQDs [34]. Additionally, the restricted geometry of CNT/CQD structures prohibits the diffusion of volatile products that might lead to the polymer chain scission. As the DTG curves in Fig. 12(b), the temperature for maximum degradation does not change with changing composition due to the low amounts of nanofillers while the degradation rate at
T max
increases for CQD and CNT/GNP/CQD reinforcement and showed a significantly low increase for CNT/CQD reinforcement when compared to PU. The result indicates better dispersion of CNT and CQDs that behave as heat barriers in the CNT/CQD hybrid.

According to Fig. 13 (a), the nanofiller included PU required higher temperatures for 5% degradation, in which increases revealed from 175 °C to 185 °C, 193 °C and 195 °C with the addition of CNT/CQD, CNT/GNP/CQD and CQD, respectively (Fig. 12(a)). Such improved early degradation temperatures indicate better interfacial interactions for the CQD and CNT/GNP/CQD reinforced PU, which delayed the initial degradation by hindering the degradation process [43]. On the other hand, degradation rates are very similar while being higher for neat PU at lower temperatures of 250 °C (Fig. 13(b)). Delayed initial degradation is attributed to the enhanced interfacial interaction between the nanofillers and the matrix [34]. Therefore, higher rates of weight percentage for nanocomposites in comparison to the neat PU are highly expected. Fig. 13(c) indicates that this behavior changed at higher temperatures and CNT/GNP/CQD reinforced PU underwent a bigger degradation at 400 °C compared to all others. Additionally, CNT/CQD reinforced PU has a significantly lower degradation rate at higher temperatures (Fig. 13 (d)). When the behavior at higher temperatures is considered, it is clear that the interactions between the matrix and CNT/CQD hybrid increase, due to sufficient dispersion.

Hybrid nanocomposites are an aspiring kind of polymer nanocomposites that eliminate the limitations imposed by agglomerations of nanofillers to achieve highly enhanced properties. This study is focused on preparing 3D nanostructures in PU matrix through 2D graphene and 1D carbon nanotubes with the increased number of cross-link points accompanied by 0D carbon quantum dots. Being highly prone to forming bindings with other nanofillers, CQDs restrain graphene stacking and CNT bundling, leading to enhanced performance. It is observed that CQDs empowered the CNT/GNP network, resulting in better dispersion and enhanced mechanical and thermal stability behavior. PU hybrid nanocomposites reinforced with binary CNT/CQD have shown the strongest tensile strength result with an improvement of 54% compared to pristine PU while the result for ternary hybrid nanocomposites with GNP/CNT/CQD has an increase of 47%. On the other hand, elastic modulus values of PU with binary CNT/CQD and ternary CNT/GNP/CQD increased by about 71% and 61%, respectively. Ternary hybrids with N:CQD has the least improvement in tensile strength (∼20%) while having one of the highest moduli (59%). Consequently, it would be extremely desirable to develop a facile and cost-efficient approach to preparing CQDs, in which the edge-abundant features of CQDs are greatly beneficial for improving dispersion rates in polymeric nanocomposites.

Amir Navidfar: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing-Original draft, Visualization, Nanocomposites Fabrications. Makbule Irmak Peker: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Nanocomposites Fabrications, Writing - Original Draft. Esranur Budak: Quantum Dots Synthesis. Caner Unlu: Supervision, Resources, Validation, Review & Editing. Levent Trabzon: Conceptualization, Validation, Resources, Review & Editing, Supervision, Administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
